frameworks.name: bounded drift-diffusion
frameworks.one_line: Evidence accumulated to a time-varying stopping bound that terminates the decision.

frameworks.name: speed-accuracy tradeoff with time cost
frameworks.one_line: Optimal policy lowers the bound over elapsed time to balance accuracy against time cost.

core_question: Does the criterion to terminate a perceptual decision become more lax over time, and is that decline modulated by the cost of time?
key_assumptions: Decisions are made by sequential evidence accumulation to a bound
Participants aim to maximize reward per unit time
Time cost can be experimentally induced via a provisional deadline
manipulations: Motion difficulty varied across trials in a random-dot direction-discrimination task
Time pressure increased midway by canceling trials exceeding a provisional deadline (<5% canceled)
Trial-by-trial points feedback for correct vs erroneous choices
key_findings: The deadline manipulation made participants faster and less accurate even though few trials were canceled
Choices and reaction times were captured by a bounded drift-diffusion model
Stopping bounds declined as a function of elapsed time within a trial in all phases
The decline in bounds was exaggerated under the higher time-cost regime
interpretations: A time-varying collapsing bound implements the optimal speed-accuracy tradeoff under time costs
Subjects adjust the urgency of decision termination in response to imposed time pressure
